coredata.dc:title: Assessment of additional aging on electric vehicles batteries in the framework of vehicle to building services
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Pelosi, D.
Brunori, E.
Barelli, L.
coredata.dc:description: The energy transition implies a progressive loss in grids inertia. Energy storage systems can contribute to enhance the flexibility of electric systems, even if their penetration in the grid at the European level currently amounts to only a few percentage points of the overall generation capacity. In this framework, the use of electric vehicles (EVs) as multiple decentralized mobile energy storage when interconnected to a bidirectional charging column for performing Vehicle-to-X services is widely proposed. In this work, as pioneering approach, the additional aging of 50-kWh BEVs and 10-kWh PHEV batteries performing Vehicle-to-Building (V2B) operation is experimentally assessed in reference to a grid-connected renewable Micro-Grid (MG). A MG dynamic model including suitable stochastic real-time power management is developed to minimize i) the instantaneous grid power exchange and, ii) the PHEV power fluctuations. Simulations outcomes provide batteries State of Charge evolutions, subsequently implemented in the design of experiment to perform accelerated aging tests. Four 18,650 NMC Li-ion cells are tested over 9 equivalent years of operation. This allows to quantify the V2B additional degradation for the investigated case study. As results, State of Health (SoH) value achieves 84.8 % for the PHEV battery (compared to 95.8 % when V2B is not performed), while it stands at 92.9 % for the BEV (slightly lower than 95 % SoH for V2B absence). These outcomes could contribute to pave the way in the awareness of V2B impact on renewable-based Micro-Grids, while reducing stressful conditions on PHEV batteries that could yield to faster degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aging
Electric vehicles
Experimental investigation
Li-ion battery
State of health
Vehicle-to-building

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamic modelling of a renewable-based micro-grid with V2B service

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Implemented procedure for assessing experimentally the additional aging due to V2B.
a) Micro-grid schematic layout and b) Simulink model implementation.
Flow chart of the Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation algorithm.
Overview of the experimental test rig.

Simulation profiles carried out on a weekly basis: BEV and PHEV powers (red and blue solid lines, respectively), b) grid power exchange (black solid line), power difference between PV generation and load demand (“DIFF”, green solid line), and c) BEV and PHEV SoCs ((red and blue dashed lines, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) BEV and PHEV powers (red and blue solid lines, respectively), in the case of no V2B services, and b) BEV and PHEV SoCs (red and blue dashed lines, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Results of the experimental campaign on NMC cells considering the V2B services.
BEV and PHEV features for V2B service.
BEV and PHEV connection times to the BCS, for the scenarios with and without V2B service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Total amount of BEV and PHEV discharged energy over the simulated week.
Application of the RFC algorithm to the weekly simulated BEV and PHEV SoCs, with and without V2B.
Registered capacities for the accelerated aging tests in the investigated scenarios.

head.ce:title: Assessment of additional aging on electric vehicles batteries in the framework of vehicle to building services

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, University of Perugia, Via G. Duranti 93, 06125 Perugia, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering, University of Perugia, Via G. Duranti 93, 06125 Perugia, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: VGA srl, Via Ugo Foscolo 1, 06053 Perugia, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: VGA srl, Via Ugo Foscolo 1, 06053 Perugia, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The energy transition implies a progressive loss in grids inertia. Energy storage systems can contribute to enhance the flexibility of electric systems, even if their penetration in the grid at the European level currently amounts to only a few percentage points of the overall generation capacity. In this framework, the use of electric vehicles (EVs) as multiple decentralized mobile energy storage when interconnected to a bidirectional charging column for performing Vehicle-to-X services is widely proposed. In this work, as pioneering approach, the additional aging of 50-kWh BEVs and 10-kWh PHEV batteries performing Vehicle-to-Building (V2B) operation is experimentally assessed in reference to a grid-connected renewable Micro-Grid (MG). A MG dynamic model including suitable stochastic real-time power management is developed to minimize i) the instantaneous grid power exchange and, ii) the PHEV power fluctuations. Simulations outcomes provide batteries State of Charge evolutions, subsequently implemented in the design of experiment to perform accelerated aging tests. Four 18,650 NMC Li-ion cells are tested over 9 equivalent years of operation. This allows to quantify the V2B additional degradation for the investigated case study. As results, State of Health (SoH) value achieves 84.8 % for the PHEV battery (compared to 95.8 % when V2B is not performed), while it stands at 92.9 % for the BEV (slightly lower than 95 % SoH for V2B absence). These outcomes could contribute to pave the way in the awareness of V2B impact on renewable-based Micro-Grids, while reducing stressful conditions on PHEV batteries that could yield to faster degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Transportation accounts for 15.8 % of the global greenhouse gas emissions in 2023 in Europe, of which 79 % due to road transportation [1]. According to European Commission targets to reach a net-zero carbon society by 2050, a huge penetration of renewables in the future grids together with transportation electrification are required. Therefore, sustainable means of transport, such as Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs), can speed up the expected European clean energy transition [1–3]. In EVs, propulsion is generally demanded by Li-ion batteries, since this kind of storage technology shows the highest energy density, good lifespan, no memory effect, and fast response [4]. On the other hand, future grids should be highly efficient and reliable to face the increasing load demand due to the BEV charging process, especially in densely populated areas [5,6]. Moreover, grids should also be renewed, to enhance renewable penetration, mainly with non-dispatchable distributed generation options [7]. Integrating such resources at the building level yields to an evolution from passive consumers of electricity to prosumers (consumers and producers), representing active agents of the electricity market. However, establishing renewable-rich future power grids should tackle with the highly intermittent, fluctuating and non-programmable production [8]. Moreover, transitioning from conventional systems to high renewable share shows challenges, as the reduction in inertia and system strength, traditionally provided by synchronous generators, leading to a loss of crucial system support functions like voltage and frequency regulation. Thus, energy storage systems, able to enhance the flexibility of the grids performing essential support services or providing postponed use of the stored energy, need to be integrated into the future grids [9,10]. This to mitigate the loss of inertia of the grids, due to reduction of synchronous generators and increase of installed electronic power converters for renewable generation causing a high rate of change of frequency, and the mismatch between production and demand [11,12].

Considering the high capital costs for installing stationary storage systems and the growing demand for BEVs and PHEVs, it is widely proposed the use of BEVs and PHEVs as multiple decentralized mobile energy storages when interconnected to a bidirectional charging column for performing Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) services, as well as Vehicle-to-Building (V2B), Vehicle-to-Home (V2H) and Vehicle-to-Load (V2L) [13]. These support services can be also provided in reverse mode (e.g. G2V, H2V or B2V). All these services represent the so-called Vehicle-to-X (V2X). For instance, a method for charging station siting and sizing in V2G, in which the service competence of the power-transportation network is jointly optimized, is studied in [14]. In [15], the optimal trip and charging scheduling of commercial electric vehicle fleets with V2B is assessed in reference to future low inertia systems, demonstrating that an optimal journey start time can increase the revenue of commercial fleets by up to 38 % in summer and 12 % in winter.

As regards V2B services, a real distribution network for evaluating the use of PHEVs for peak-shaving, loss reduction and voltage profile improvement, is modelled in [16], while [17] demonstrated the benefits of BEVs and PHEVs integration as dynamically configurable distributed energy storage acting in V2B operating mode. [18] studies the optimal configurations of the number of EVs interacting with a grid-connected commercial building by examining the impact of EVs number on the daily electricity bills. Moreover, the range of allowable EV numbers enabling optimal V2B capacity and reasonable charging loads for reducing the costs of building energy management, and the role of different EV numbers in affecting the stationary battery capacity in an isolated commercial building microgrid, are investigated. A novel approach of combined V2H and V2B use applied to different scenarios, as when the building workers own EVs, company shared car fleets or leasing, is presented in [19]. However, to maximize the benefits of BEVs/PHEVs use for V2X services, proper suitable power management strategies are required, also considering the additional aging introduced by such services and the increasing complexity of decentralized power systems as smart grids [20]. In this context, different power management strategies for V2B integration into smart-grids and renewable-based grids are shown in [22–26].

Nevertheless, quantifying the additional degradation of BEV and PHEV batteries employed for V2X services is not widely investigated in literature. For instance, [26] quantifies the EV battery degradation across six charging strategies by means of experimental tests carried out on 18,650 NCA cells and the development of an aging model. A rule-based simulation model is implemented to simulate EV operation. However, they mainly focus on six different BEV charging strategies, i.e. immediate charging, late charging, increase of self-consumption, frequency containment reserve, positive automatic frequency restoration reserve, and intraday market trading, demonstrating that V2X does not reduce significantly the battery State of Health (SoH). Furthermore, [26] presents limitations since V2B effects on PHEVs are not considered and the investigated time horizon of 20 months results in a limited fraction of BEV expected lifetime. Thus, quantitative information on SoH degradation is not so significant due to the nonlinearity of capacity fading over cycling. Moreover, at the best of our knowledge, no quantitative comparison studies on the additional aging induced by V2B on EVs batteries are presented in literature to date.

In contrast to previous studies, this research presents, as innovative contribution, an experimental evaluation of the battery additional aging caused by V2B operation, with respect to normal use for vehicle propulsion, within a specific case study. This considers the integration of both PHEV and BEV into a grid-connected renewable Micro-Grid (MG) consisting of a PV system installed to a residential building (three apartments). Specifically, accelerated tests, carried out on 18,650 NMC Li-ion cells covering 9 equivalent years of operation, are designed based on the outcomes of a developed dynamic model by the authors in [27]. The model also includes a proper Power Management Strategy (PMS) based on Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation (SPSA), implemented for minimizing: i) the instantaneous power exchange with the grid and, ii) the power fluctuation managed by PHEV battery. These objectives answer the need of increasing MG self-consumption while reducing stress on the PHEV battery, which would be more solicitated in terms of operating C-rates and Depth of Discharges (DoDs) than BEV due to the lower battery capacity. Therefore, the main objective of the present work is to assess the V2B additional degradation with respect to V2B absence over the expected lifespan of the BEV and PHEV batteries.

As demonstrated in the following, after 9 equivalent years of operation related to the specific case study, the PHEV battery shows an increase of about 11 percentage points in terms of additional aging with respect to PHEV not performing V2B service (i.e., capacity fading results in 84.8 % vs. 95.8 % of the initial capacity). Otherwise, it is evident that in case of BEV, due to the higher installed capacity and, consequently, lower operating C-rates and DoDs, only 2 percentage points of additional capacity fading (i.e., 92.9 % vs. 95 %) is registered when BEV performs V2B with respect to the case of exclusive battery pack exploitation for propulsion.

These results can provide useful awareness on V2B benefits in enhancing grid stability and increasing self-consumption, while reducing stressful conditions on PHEV batteries that could yield to a faster degradation.
The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 describes the micro-grid modelling and the design of experiment, Section 3 the experimental set-up, while Section 4 reports the main results of the study.

The implemented procedure for assessing PHEV and BEV additional aging is illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, the MG dynamic model detailed in Section 2.1 is developed to provide as output the weekly SoC evolutions relating to PHEV and BEV performing V2B, respectively. Moreover, to compare the additional aging due to V2B, PHEV and BEV SoC trends are simulated in the case of V2B service absence (i.e., normal BEV and PHEV battery operation for propulsion). Each resulting profile (i.e., SoC evolutions for BEV and PHEV with and without V2B service) is then repeated 52 times to obtain profiles of annual SoC evolution. Second, the Rain Flow cycle Counting (RFC) algorithm is applied to the four SoC profiles, obtaining the frequency of cycle per DoD class over the year, as detailed in the following Section 2.2. Third, to accelerate testing time, the experimental campaign is designed reproducing the obtained annual distribution of cycles' frequency per DoD class at cell level. This is done by scaling charging and discharging current values to the maximum continuous charge and discharge currents, as specified by the manufacturer for the selected NMC Li-ion cells. In this way the test campaign relative to one equivalent year of operation is defined. As regards the experimental campaign, the four selected cells are initially characterized by means of Battery Capacity Determination (BCD) technique, and the cycling procedure is repeated emulating three equivalent years of operation. At the end of the three years, BCD is performed again. The overall procedure is repeated to achieve 9 equivalent years of operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic modelling of a renewable-based micro-grid with V2B service

ce:section.ce:para: The present study aims to experimentally investigate the additional battery aging due to vehicle to building services, moving from the outcomes of the paper presented by the authors in [27]. Specifically, a residential renewable-based grid-connected micro-grid (MG) was investigated, also including a 50-kWh BEV and a 10-kWh PHEV through a bidirectional connection to the building, consisting of three apartments equipped with 11-kWp photovoltaic (PV) system. A dynamic model of the considered MG was carried out, and the power shares were instantaneously computed by means of a multi-objective power management strategy based on SPSA algorithm. MG schematic view and model implementation are depicted in Fig. 2 .

As evident from Fig. 2, SPSA algorithm instantaneously calculates the power shares (resulting as the instantaneous difference between the PV generation and the load) among the electric vehicles and the grid. This is done in reference to a specific multi-objective performance function, also considering the imposed technical constraints, such as battery capacities, maximum/minimum C-rates, which are listed in Table 1 . Specifically, 0.5C is chosen as the threshold within which C-rate can vary during the V2B operation both in charge and discharge. This value is selected to limit the battery exploitation for the V2B service with respect to the priority exploitation for propulsion. Moreover, 90 % is chosen as the maximum allowed DoD, as commonly set to limit Li-ion battery discharge to avoid a fast degradation.

The algorithm inputs are BEV and PHEV current battery State of Charge (SoC), the instantaneous values of PV generation and load demand, as well as the power share assigned to the PHEV battery at the previous time step, with the purpose of allocating the optimal power shares considering the pursed objectives as detailed in the following. As renewable generation, 11-kWp PV system installed in a building placed in Central-Southern Italy was considered [28]. The user load demand was deduced from a gathered real dataset (relating to 20 apartments) continuously acquired for two years, as indicated in [29]. The implemented load relates to a complex residential user composed of three apartments. Dynamic simulations aim to investigate the effects of the integration of two different kinds of vehicles (i.e., PHEV and BEV) in performing V2B services. Specifically, simulations provide optimized power profiles related to the grid, and BEV and PHEV terminals. BEV and PHEV batteries are modelled in Simulink environment through real data measurements of open circuit voltages and internal resistances at varying SoC, gathered during both charging and discharging processes, implemented in four different look-up tables. This to emulate the actual behaviour of BEV and PHEV batteries. Further details on the battery modelling are presented in a previous work of the authors [30]. Initial SoCs were fixed at 100 % for both BEV and PHEV. The MG behaviour was analysed over a week as time horizon. BEV and PHEV is connected to the Bidirectional Charging Station (BCS) from 7 PM to 8 AM each of the working days (from Monday, corresponding to the simulation start, to Friday). Subsequently, BEV comes back home at 7 PM in the working days, while PHEV returns with a lowered SoC at 1 PM and leaves the building at 3 PM restoring the maximum SoC through PV surplus. In the latter case, it is assumed that the PHEV is driven in full-electric mode. On the other hand, BEV and PHEV are continuously connected at the BCS on Saturday and Sunday. Table 2 lists the daily connections to the building over the week for the two investigated scenarios.

Power management was developed and implemented based on SPSA algorithm, aiming at the minimization of both the instantaneous power exchange with the grid and the power fluctuation at PHEV terminals, according to the targets of the multi-objective performance function. Section 2.1.2 provides further details relative to SPSA algorithm and PMS formulation.

To compare the additional aging introduced by V2B, simulations are also realized considering BEV and PHEV not performing V2B, with only two charges per week. It results that energy withdrawal from the grid is reduced of −31.8 % (from 417.7 kWh to 285.1 kWh obtained without and with V2B, respectively), while PV energy surplus delivered to grid decreases of −80.2 % (from 112.4 kWh to 22.2 kWh).

As already demonstrated in [31], SPSA algorithm represents an effective stochastic optimization method to be used in control applications. Such an algorithm, introduced by Spall in 1992, is a fast convergent option in the context of global optimization problems of an unknown functional form relating to system performances [32]. SPSA is a very effective algorithm in a wide range of practical applications, especially for the ones characterized by significant stochastic behaviour as in the case of non-programmable renewable sources. This is due to features as ease of implementation, robustness to noise in the loss measurements, no need of loss function gradient and explicit mathematical representation of uncertainty, and ability to find global minimum, as indicated in [35–39]. Hence, SPSA is a very suitable tool for Li-ion batteries being subjected to fast degradation under stressful charging/discharging conditions [38–41], as in V2B services. Therefore, the benefits provided by SPSA algorithm implementation are already widely demonstrated in literature. SPSA implementation is summarized in Fig. 3 , considering 10, 0.601, 0.101 as set values for A, α, γ parameters, as indicated in [26].

Problem formulation related to the investigated case study requires the definition of:
-
the vector of unknown parameters ( θ ), composed of three dimensionless shares (i.e. PHEV, BEV and grid, namely
q PHEV
,
q BEV
and
q GRID
) of the instantaneous difference between the PV generation and load, as indicated by (1).

q BEV
and
q GRID
parameters vary between 0 and 1.

θ =
q PHEV
q BEV
q GRID

Hence, (2) shows the power shares according to the computed split factors.
(2)

where ∆ P is the instantaneous difference between the PV generation and load.
-
The performance multi-objective function, considering the following targets:
1.
Smooth PHEV battery profile, by means of the ratio between the power imposed to the PHEV at the timestep t and the managed power at the previous instant (t-1), as indicated in (3).
(3)

2.
Minimize the power profile exchanged with the grid, as in (4).
(4)

The multi-objective problem is solved by means of the weighted sum of the selected objectives, calculated until the convergence condition or the maximum number of iterations was reached, as described by (5).
(5)

The two weights
w 1
and

are set at a value of 0.5. Finally, vector θ was computed considering the initial SoCs of BEV and PHEV, according to (6).
(6)

0.02

Simulated results are implemented to build the experimental campaign. Specifically, BEV and PHEV SoCs obtained from simulation are considered to carry out the experiments. Moving from BEV and PHEV weekly SoCs, the Rain Flow cycle Counting (RFC) algorithm, widely used to estimate the lifespan of the batteries, is applied to identify all fragmented cycles and assess for each battery the frequency of cycles per classes of DoD. It is further assumed that cycles with a DoD lower than 5 % can be neglected.

The weekly frequency of cycles per classes of DoD is determined for both BEV and PHEV, considering for each battery two operating scenarios, i.e., with and without V2B service. Moreover, for both scenarios, obtained weekly frequency of cycles per classes of DoD is repeated 52 times to assess the number of cycles per DoD class representing the battery solicitation for each equivalent year of operation. To provide a reference for comparison, the same procedure is applied, for both BEV and PHEV, on the SoC evolution obtained by simulation for the case of V2B absence. Therefore, a total of four fresh cells are tested.

Each galvanostatic charge and discharge cycle is performed at the maximum continuous charging and discharging currents of 6 A and 20 A, respectively, to obtain an accelerated aging. It is noteworthy that the maximum discharge current is limited with respect to the datasheet (i.e., 35 A) because the current limitations of the instrument (max 20 A). Experimental campaign is carried out to emulate 9 equivalent years of operation, repeating the weekly test design.

At the beginning, an initial characterization of the four considered cells is performed to evaluate the nominal capacities. Moreover, every three equivalent years of operation, the capacities of the cells are assessed.

The accelerated aging test campaign was carried out on four Li-ion Nickel-Manganese-Cobalt (NMC) 18650 P26A cells, purchased from MOLICEL®. These cells have a nominal capacity of 2600 mAh, operating within a voltage range of 2.5–4.2 V, with maximum charge and discharge currents of 6 A and 35 A, respectively. The specifications of the cells considered can be found in [42]. It is highlighted that they are characterized by a significant tolerance to cycling. The equipment BTS4000-5V20A battery testing system is an 8-channels instrument, with 0-5 V as voltage range and ±20 A as maximum current, as indicated in [43]. Instrument accuracy is of 0.1 % of full scale both in terms of voltage and current. The device was purchased from NEWARE©. Cells were maintained at a fixed temperature of 20 ± 1 °C during the tests. The acquisition frequency is set at 1 Hz. The experimental test rig is depicted in Fig. 4 .

Simulations were carried out in reference to a weekly scenario. Fig. 5 illustrates the main parameters obtained from simulations in reference to the implemented power management strategy including V2B services. Simulations started at 7 PM, corresponding to the daily arrival time of BEV and PHEV at home. PHEV is assumed to perform four daily drive cycles, each one of 11.6 km as mileage. Specifically, the SC03 Supplemental federal Test Procedure (SFTP) urban test drive was considered as PHEV drive cycle. The daily drive PHEV cycle was obtained by repeating two times the SC03 test cycle. PHEV was assumed to leave the house at 7 AM, coming back for lunch at 1 PM where it was completely charged (as visible from Fig. 5) and leaving the house at 3 PM. Finally, PHEV comes back home at 7 PM, starting to perform V2B services. The resulting PHEV daily mileage corresponds to about 46 km. As concerns BEV, it is assumed as daily drive cycle the extra urban SFTP US06 drive cycle (12.8 km as mileage), with only one daily departure and arrival. The total BEV mileage is equal to 76.8 km, obtained by repeating three times the US06 cycle twice a day. As evident from Fig. 5, BEV requires two full charges and a partial charge three times a week.

The obtained BEV and PHEV power trends are then compared with the cases of BEV and PHEV power and SoC profiles (see Fig. 6 ) not performing V2B services. It is highlighted from Fig. 6 that BEV and PHEV are fully charged at the beginning of day 2 and day 4 with a constant power of 5 kW, guaranteeing the mileage according to the drivers' uses. It is highlighted as the negative powers correspond to the charging phases (regeneration phases during driving, steady charge at the charging point), and the positive powers are related to the discharge due to the driving.

By an energetic point of view, the total amount of BEV and PHEV discharged energy over the simulated week corresponds to a) 98.3 kWh for BEV without V2B, b) 133.7 kWh for BEV with V2B, c) 18.6 kWh for PHEV without V2B, and d) 50.5 kWh for PHEV with V2B. These numbers, reported in Table 3 , highlight for both PHEV and BEV batteries the relevance of the V2B service with respect to the propulsion operation.

BEV and PHEV SoC evolutions deduced from simulations are then used for the following design of experiment. This aims to investigate, according to the specific profile applied to PHEV and BEV batteries, the additional aging introduced by performing V2B services over the specific residential building taken into account.

Based on BEV and PHEV weekly SoC evolutions obtained from simulations (Figs. 5 and 6), the Rain Flow cycle Counting (RFC) algorithm is applied to identify all fragmented cycles. The resulting absolute frequency per DoD class is reported in Table 4 for the four investigated cases, i.e. BEV, PHEV with and without V2B service.

For each of the four cases of Table 4, a new cell is subjected to the specific accelerated aging test. As already detailed, galvanostatic charge and discharge cycles are performed at the maximum continuous charging and discharging currents of 6 A and 20 A, respectively. Weekly test design is repeated up to 9 equivalent years of operation.

Capacity fading data, as resulting from capacity measurements performed on the 4 tested cells at the beginning and every three equivalent years of operation, are listed in Table 5 . As evident from Table 5 and Fig. 7 , a relevant reduction in terms of capacity fading, reaching less than 85 % of the initial capacity at the end of the 9-equivalent year, is registered in case of PHEV performing V2B services. This is mainly due to the lower capacity of the PHEV battery (i.e., 10 kWh) and the higher number of recharges by exploiting PV surplus at lunch time during working days.

Different considerations can be done for BEV. A lower residual capacity is measured for BEV in case of V2B, with respect to the case of V2B absence, because of the further solicitations aiming at minimizing the grid power exchanges. Nevertheless, capacity lowers only of about 2 additional percentage points with respect to the absence of V2B service.

This work aims to quantify the additional aging introduced by V2B service on both PHEV and BEV batteries, into a grid-connected renewable Micro-Grid (MG) consisting of a PV system installed to a residential building (three apartments) managed by a suitable PMS based on SPSA. This aims to minimize the instantaneous power exchange with the grid and the power fluctuations managed by PHEV battery. It results that energy withdrawal from the grid and PV energy surplus delivered to grid is reduced of −31.8 % and −80.2 %, respectively, with respect to the case the V2B service is not provided. The batteries additional aging corresponding to such V2B service is experimentally assessed. Experimental campaign carried out on four 18650 NMC Li-ion cells demonstrates that, in reference to the investigated case study, performing V2B by PHEV produces a significant additional degradation of about 11 percentage points. This is due to the excessive PHEV battery exploitation due to V2B operation after 9 equivalent years, with respect to PHEV not performing V2B (i.e., measured SoH values of 84.8 % vs. 95.8 %, respectively). Such degradation underlines the importance of developing suitable management strategies to minimize the degradation when PHEV perform V2B services. On the other hand, BEV higher installed capacity allows to operate at lower operating C-rates, leading to an additional capacity fading of only 2 percentage points (i.e., 92.9 % vs. 95 %) when operating V2B with respect to the case of V2B absence.

These findings validate the simulation results and provide scientific evidence on the benefits of utilizing battery electric vehicles in V2X services. This includes supporting the grid and/or maximizing the self-consumption of energy in commercial, industrial or residential buildings, within the context of the anticipated growth of renewable energy in future power grids. Although the PHEV battery exhibits the most significant capacity fading, the power management strategy based on SPSA enables the battery to also perform Vehicle-to-Building (V2B) services throughout the typical lifespan guaranteed by EV manufacturers (i.e., 80 % SoH threshold).

Consequently, these results can raise valuable awareness concerning the integration of V2B services into renewable-based microgrids. This integration can enhance grid stability and increase self-consumption while mitigating stressful conditions on PHEV batteries that could lead to accelerated degradation.

Future outlooks will aim to extend the investigation to other case studies (e.g. grid support services) and EV battery chemistries, such as LFP, NCA and Na-ion, to evaluate the degree of V2X induced degradation. This to provide useful results for users and manufacturers in performing such kinds of services, and to estimate their economic viability, also considering possible future remuneration schemes for providing V2X services.

D. Pelosi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. E. Brunori: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. L. Barelli: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
